basic_info.incident_name_cn: 乔治亚太平洋公司硫化氢中毒事故

process_context.operating_conditions: ambient temperature and pressure; anaerobic decomposition in confined sewer infrastructure

failure_mechanism.root_cause: Failure to recognize and control hydrogen sulfide generation during anaerobic decomposition of sulfide-laden wastewater in confined sewer spaces.
failure_mechanism.deviation_type: leakage and accumulation of toxic gas
failure_mechanism.causal_chain: Inadequate hazard assessment of wastewater chemistry → anaerobic sulfide oxidation in sewer → uncontrolled H2S generation → lack of ventilation and gas monitoring → H2S accumulation in manhole → contractor entry without detection or PPE → acute inhalation exposure and fatalities
failure_mechanism.physical_principles: Gas solubility equilibrium, anaerobic microbial metabolism, density-driven stratification of H2S (heavier than air), confined space atmospheric stagnation

protection_system.safeguard_failures: Permit system did not require real-time H2S monitoring; ventilation was not verified effective prior to entry; training failed to address site-specific H2S generation potential from process wastewater
csb_recommendations: Implement mandatory continuous direct-reading H2S monitoring before and during confined space entry
Conduct reactive hazard reviews for all waste streams containing sulfur species
Require emergency response plans specific to H2S incidents, including immediate evacuation and non-entry rescue protocols

embedding_text: At Georgia-Pacific's Naheola mill, contractors entered a sewer manhole during tank truck unloading of sulfide-containing wastewater. Anaerobic decomposition generated high-concentration hydrogen sulfide (H2S) that accumulated due to poor ventilation and lack of real-time monitoring. The deviation was excessive toxic gas concentration (More H2S) caused by unrecognized reactive hazard in waste treatment. Root cause: failure to identify H2S generation potential in confined infrastructure. Result: two deaths and eight injuries from acute inhalation exposure.
